Okay, so I'm trying to figure out whether the mutant code change in DateTimeZone.java is causing any adjacent faults. The mutant changes lines 88-93 as follows:

Original:
if (minutesOffset < 0 || minutesOffset > 59) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException(...);
}
int offset = 0;
try {

Mutant:
if (true) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException(...);
}
int offset = 0;
try {

So the mutant is replacing the condition that checks if minutesOffset is within 0-59 with a constant true condition. That means, regardless of the value of minutesOffset, it will always throw an exception if this block is reached.

Looking at the Failing Tests Information, there's one test that changed status from failing to passing: TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear. But for other tests in TestDateTimeZoneCutover, their statuses didn't change or they still failed but with different messages.

The key here is whether this mutant introduces any adjacent faults. An adjacent fault would be a new bug introduced by the mutation that wasn't covered by the original failing test. In this case, when the minutesOffset check is replaced with a constant true, it's possible that other parts of the code that rely on valid minute values are now breaking, leading to additional failures.

The TestDateTimeZoneCutover tests seem to be affected because they were expecting certain behaviors but now get an IllegalArgumentException due to the mutant. This indicates that the change isn't just fixing one issue but might be causing new ones elsewhere.

Therefore, this mutation does introduce adjacent faults because it's breaking other parts of the system beyond what was intended.
</think>
